text: The surgeon aligns the resected ends of the bowel, resembling two rosebuds, using interrupted sutures to create a tension-free anastomosis.

The surgeon performs an indocyanine green test to assess the quality of the anastomosis. Two 2-0 Vicryl sutures are used in the center, followed by a transition stitch at the corner, ensuring durability during the procedure.

The surgeon uses a hand-sewn technique to deliver and pull up on the suture with the left hand, carefully walking it up and setting it with the needle. At the corner, a locking stitch is placed to prevent a purse string effect, followed by another stitch before starting from the opposite side. The needle is set with small motions to maintain efficient movement.

The surgeon performs a transition stitch using a loaded needle, ensuring proper tension and loop setting by utilizing the top of the curve on the front side of the needle. The needle tip is used to set the suture, meeting the last suture with the first from the corner, while taking full thickness bites.

The surgeon ties off the suture line, ensuring it sits smoothly, and places additional lumbers to relieve tension from the staple line. The procedure involves extensive sewing and the use of slip knots.
The surgeon closes the remaining part of the jejunostomy site that was not addressed by the tinea, using sutures to secure the corner. A leak test is performed to ensure the integrity of the closure.
